data.customTitle: What should a PR review checklist include for an ML model...

inputState.inputText: What should a PR review checklist include for an ML model change?

message.text: What should a PR review checklist include for an ML model change?

response.value:
Per §5.1 of the Brambles DS Standards: A PR checklist for an ML model change must verify experiment tracking, reproducibility, tests, documentation (model card), registry lifecycle, monitoring, and security before merge.

Checklist (short)
- Experiment tracking: MLflow run(s) exist with Git SHA and dataset version tagged. (§5.1)
- Model card: training data, features, hyperparams, metrics, limitations, intended use documented. (§3.3)
- Tests: unit + integration + data validation tests (pytest); aim for coverage and passing CI. (§4.3)
- Reproducibility: pinned deps (requirements.txt or environment.yml), deterministic seeds, training/inference parity. (§6.4)
- Registry & deployment: model registered in MLflow/Databricks with stage, SLA, and rollback plan. (§5.2, §1.3)
- Performance & fairness: benchmark on holdout and slices, fairness/bias checks, privacy/PII review. (§5.4, §7.5)
- CI checks: linting, security scans, no secrets in code. (§2.1, §4.5)
- Ops readiness: monitoring config, alerts, runbook, retraining trigger defined. (§5.4, §1.4)

What to do
- Log every training run to MLflow and tag with Git SHA.
- Add or update the model card in docs/.
- Run pytest and include data validation tests.
- Pin dependencies in requirements.txt or environment.yml.
- Register the artifact in MLflow/Databricks and set rollback stage.
- Execute benchmark and fairness tests; document results.
- Remove secrets and add any needed secret references to Secrets Manager.
- Create monitoring dashboards and a support runbook.

Per §5.1, §3.3, §4.3, §5.2, §5.4, §4.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: How should we version control feature engineering logic?

inputState.inputText: How should we version control feature engineering logic?

message.text: How should we version control feature engineering logic?

response.value:
Direct answer: Keep feature engineering as versioned, production-grade code (packaged, tested, and traceable), not ad‑hoc notebooks.

What to do:
1. Organize: create src/<project>/features as a pip-installable package.  
2. Document: add docstrings, a features README, and update the model card.  
3. Package: add setup.py/pyproject to publish internally.  
4. Version: use Git (feature/* branches), tag releases with semantic versions and include Git SHA in MLflow runs.  
5. Track data: enable S3 versioning or DVC for input datasets and record dataset refs in MLflow.  
6. Test: add pytest unit tests and data-validation tests (Great Expectations).  
7. Automate: add CI to run Black, linters, tests; require PR review before merge.  
8. Catalog: publish feature package to internal registry and register in central feature catalog/shared library.

Named tools: GitHub/Bitbucket, MLflow, S3 or DVC, pytest, Great Expectations, Black.  
Per §6.3, §5.1, and §4.3 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What coding standards should DS team members follow for p...

inputState.inputText: What coding standards should DS team members follow for production code?

message.text: What coding standards should DS team members follow for production code?

response.value:
Direct answer: Production code must follow PEP 8-style formatting (Black 88 char), use type hints, follow the standard project layout, include docstrings, have ≥80% unit-test coverage, pass automated linters/CI, require PR peer review, and never hardcode secrets.

What to do:
1. Configure Black (line-length=88) and add pre-commit hooks to format and lint.
2. Add PEP‑484 type hints to all production functions.
3. Create the standard repo layout (src/, tests/, docs/, configs/).
4. Add NumPy/Google-style docstrings for public APIs.
5. Write pytest unit/integration tests (target ≥80% coverage) and add CI to run them and linters.
6. Enforce PRs with ≥1 reviewer and passing CI.
7. Move secrets to AWS Secrets Manager / SSM / Bitbucket Repo Variables / Databricks Secrets and remove from code.
8. Use GitHub Copilot only for assistance and review any AI-generated code.

Per §2.1–2.4, §3.1, §4.2–4.5, §5.1, §6.4 of the Brambles DS Standards.

inputState.inputText: How should we handle experiment tracking for hyperparameter tuning?

message.text: How should we handle experiment tracking for hyperparameter tuning?

response.value:
Running a standards-backed answer lookup (using the Brambles DS Standards skill) to produce a concise, actionable plan for hyperparameter tuning experiment tracking. Calling the standards skill and reporting intent in parallel.Direct answer: Use MLflow (Databricks-backed) to log every hyperparameter-tuning trial (parameters, metrics, artifacts, dataset version, code SHA), group trials with a parent run, and register the chosen model in MLflow Model Registry for reproducibility and promotion.

What to do:
- Configure MLflow tracking URI to Databricks and create a tuning experiment.
- Create a parent run; for each trial start a child run.
- Log params with mlflow.log_param(), metrics with mlflow.log_metric(), artifacts/checkpoints with mlflow.log_artifact().
- Log dataset version/reference and Git SHA; log environment spec (conda/requirements).
- Register best run via MLflow Model Registry.
- Store artifacts in S3/Delta and enable Databricks/Grafana dashboards for monitoring and retraining triggers.

Per §5.1, §5.2 and §6.4 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What requirements does the standards doc place on classif...

inputState.inputText: What requirements does the standards doc place on classification model evaluation?

message.text: What requirements does the standards doc place on classification model evaluation?

response.value:
Direct answer: Classification models must be evaluated with pre-registered hypotheses and power analysis, logged and reproducible experiment runs, documented performance & fairness in a model card, automated tests and data-validation, versioned model promotion via a model registry, and production monitoring with drift/retraining rules.

What to do (actionable steps):
- Define evaluation metrics and thresholds (precision, recall, AUC, calibration) and sample-size via power analysis (≥80% power, α=0.05).  
- Run evaluations and log runs: mlflow.start_run(); mlflow.log_param()/log_metric()/log_artifact(); tag Git SHA and dataset version.  
- Create a model card with training data, hyperparams, benchmarks, fairness, limitations.  
- Add pytest unit/integration tests and data checks (e.g., Great Expectations).  
- Register/promote models in Databricks/MLflow Model Registry with approval gates.  
- Enable monitoring (Evidently AI, CloudWatch, Databricks dashboards) and define retraining triggers.

Per §1.5, §5.1, §3.3, §4.3, §5.2, §5.4 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: How should we compare a candidate model against the curre...

inputState.inputText: How should we compare a candidate model against the current production baseline?

message.text: How should we compare a candidate model against the current production baseline?

response.value:
Direct answer: Compare by (1) identical offline evaluation, (2) statistically powered online experiments (shadow / A/B / canary), and (3) staged promotion when monitored production metrics meet predefined thresholds — all tracked in MLflow and the Model Registry with a rollback plan.

What to do:
1. Create an MLflow experiment; run mlflow.start_run(), mlflow.log_param(), mlflow.log_metric(), mlflow.log_artifact(); tag run with Git SHA. (Tools: MLflow)  
2. Evaluate both models on the same versioned test set; compute accuracy/AUC, calibration, precision/recall, latency, throughput, resource use, and fairness metrics.  
3. Run power analysis; design A/B with ≥80% power at α=0.05 and pre-register hypotheses. (Per §1.5)  
4. Run a shadow deployment or canary via Databricks Model Serving or containerized service; route small traffic slice. (Tools: Databricks Model Serving, ECS/EKS)  
5. Monitor data drift, performance drift, latency, and errors with Evidently AI + CloudWatch/Grafana; set automated pass/fail thresholds and alerting. (Tools: Evidently AI, CloudWatch)  
6. Capture production predictions and outcomes; log for retraining and feedback loops.  
7. Register candidate in MLflow Model Registry; promote to Production only after thresholds pass and tests (unit/integration via pytest, data checks via Great Expectations) succeed.

7. Register candidate in MLflow Model Registry; promote to Production only after thresholds pass and tests (unit/integration via pytest, data checks via Great Expectations) succeed.  
8. Create a model card, runbook, and rollback plan before promotion.

Per §1.5, §5.1–§5.5, and §4.3 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: How should secrets, credentials, and configuration be man...

inputState.inputText: How should secrets, credentials, and configuration be managed in data science code and pipelines?

message.text: How should secrets, credentials, and configuration be managed in data science code and pipelines?

response.value:
Direct answer: Store secrets outside code and inject them at runtime from managed stores (AWS Secrets Manager, SSM Parameter Store, Bitbucket Repository Variables, or Databricks Secrets); never hardcode credentials or commit them to Git. Per §4.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

What to do:
- Create secrets in AWS Secrets Manager/SSM or Databricks Secrets.
- Add CI secrets as Bitbucket Repository Variables.
- Assign least-privilege IAM roles to services and Databricks clusters.
- Read secrets via cloud SDKs or Databricks Secrets API; do not log them.
- Exclude secret-containing config files from VCS; add config.example templates.
- Enable rotation, audit logging (CloudTrail) and document rotation/runbook procedures.

Per §4.5 (and §6.4) of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What monitoring should be in place before a model goes live?

inputState.inputText: What monitoring should be in place before a model goes live?

message.text: What monitoring should be in place before a model goes live?

response.value:
Direct answer: Put monitoring in place for data drift, model-performance drift, system health (latency/error rates/resource use), business KPIs, logging/tracing, alerting, retraining triggers, dashboards, runbooks and a rollback plan before go‑live.

What to do:
1. Define SLAs (latency p50/p95, throughput, availability).  
2. Instrument model: log inputs, predictions, confidences, timestamps, request IDs to MLflow or logs.  
3. Enable data-quality checks and schema validation with Great Expectations.  
4. Set drift & performance checks (Evidently AI or Databricks) and thresholds.  
5. Create dashboards in CloudWatch/Databricks/Grafana/PowerBI showing metrics + business KPIs.  
6. Configure alerts (threshold + trend) to PagerDuty/Slack/email.  
7. Define retraining triggers, retention, and feedback pipeline to store labels.  
8. Publish runbook, model card, and rollback procedure to Support.

Per §5.4, §5.5, §1.3, §1.4, §4.3 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: How should we handle model rollback if prediction quality...

inputState.inputText: How should we handle model rollback if prediction quality degrades?

message.text: How should we handle model rollback if prediction quality degrades?

response.value:
Direct answer: Roll back by reverting to a previously-validated model version via the MLflow/Databricks Model Registry and an approved rollback plan whenever monitoring shows sustained SLA or prediction-quality breaches.

What to do (actionable steps)
1. Define thresholds: set quality/SLA thresholds and drift rules in monitoring (Evidently AI, CloudWatch, Databricks dashboards).  
2. Detect & alert: enable automatic alerts and create a Jira incident when thresholds are crossed.  
3. Triage: run quick validation tests (smoke/accuracy checks) against recent labeled data.  
4. Revert: promote the last known-good model from MLflow/Databricks Model Registry to production stage.  
5. Deploy safely: use Databricks Model Serving or containerized canary deployment (shadow/canary) and run smoke tests.  
6. Notify & document: update the model card, runbook, and Jira ticket; escalate per runbook.  
7. Remediate: enable retraining or rollback-to-retrain pipelines, capture predictions/outcomes for feedback.  
8. Audit: log Git SHA, metrics, and the rollback decision for postmortem.

Named tools: MLflow/Databricks Model Registry, Databricks Model Serving, Evidently AI, AWS CloudWatch, Jira, and the model card/runbook artifacts.

Per §1.3, §1.4, §5.2, §5.4 and §5.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What CI/CD pipeline stages are required for ML model depl...

inputState.inputText: What CI/CD pipeline stages are required for ML model deployment?

message.text: What CI/CD pipeline stages are required for ML model deployment?

response.value:
Per §4.4 and §5.3 of the Brambles DS Standards: an ML CI/CD pipeline must span build/test, experiment & data validation, model packaging/registry, secure deployment (canary/shadow), monitoring/rollback, and retraining feedback.

What to do (stages — action verbs):
1. Configure repo: add linting, pre-commit hooks, and Bitbucket Pipelines CI to run builds.  
2. Run tests: execute unit tests (pytest) and integration tests; enforce ≥80% coverage.  
3. Validate data/experiments: run Great Expectations checks and log runs to MLflow (mlflow.start_run(), log_param/metric/artifact).  
4. Evaluate & approve: run automated model quality/fairness checks (Evidently), then require human approval in PR.  
5. Package & register: package model artifact and register in MLflow/Databricks Model Registry; add a model card (README + model_card).  
6. Secure secrets: store creds in AWS Secrets Manager / SSM / Bitbucket repo variables; add secret-scan step.  
7. Deploy: create canary/shadow deployment via Databricks Model Serving, Kubeflow, or ECS/EKS; automate infra via Terraform.  
8. Monitor & rollback: enable drift/performance alerts (Evidently, CloudWatch, Grafana) and implement automatic/manual rollback procedures.  
9. Retrain & capture: log predictions/outcomes to storage (S3/Delta), schedule retraining pipelines and human approval gates.

Named tools: Bitbucket Pipelines, pytest, Black/pre-commit, MLflow, Databricks Model Registry/Model Serving, Kubeflow, ECS/EKS, Terraform, Great Expectations, Evidently AI, AWS Secrets Manager/SSM/Bitbucket variables.

Per §4.4 and §5.3 of the Brambles DS Standards.

Per §4.3 and §7.1 of the Brambles DS Standards: training data must be versioned, quality-validated, and subject to automated data-validation and annotation-QA before use in experiments or production.

data.customTitle: What review and approval steps must happen before a model...

inputState.inputText: What review and approval steps must happen before a model is registered in the Model Registry?

message.text: What review and approval steps must happen before a model is registered in the Model Registry?

response.value:
Per §5.2 of the Brambles DS Standards, a model may only be registered after experiments are MLflow‑logged, a complete model card exists, tests and CI pass, SLAs and a rollback plan are defined, monitoring/runbooks are in place, and formal peer/application‑development approvals (senior review for high‑stakes) are obtained.

What to do (action steps)
- Log: record the run in MLflow (mlflow.start_run()), log params/metrics/artifacts, tag Git SHA and dataset version.  
- Create: a model card documenting data, features, hyperparams, benchmarks, fairness, intended use, limitations.  
- Run: unit, integration, and data‑validation tests (pytest, Great Expectations).  
- Set up: CI that enforces linting/tests/security checks before merge.  
- Define: SLAs (latency/throughput/availability) and a rollback plan.  
- Create: runbooks and monitoring dashboards (Evidently AI, Databricks dashboards, CloudWatch/Grafana).  
- Obtain: peer DS review (≥1), App Dev sign‑off, Support/ops handoff; require senior/staff DS approval for high‑stakes models.  
- Register: publish the artifact to the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry using its approval workflow.

Per §5.2 (see also §5.1, §3.3, §1.3, §1.5, §4.2 of the Brambles DS Standards).

data.customTitle: What is the role of Jira in the data science workflow?

inputState.inputText: What is the role of Jira in the data science workflow?

message.text: What is the role of Jira in the data science workflow?

response.value:
Jira is the single system of record for planning, tracking, and governing data‑science work—project charters, time‑boxed experiments, epics/stories/spikes, and production incidents—ensuring traceability between work, code, and PRs.

What to do:
- Create the project charter in Jira (business question, approach, data sources, KPIs).
- Time-box exploratory work and record each experiment as a Jira task/story.
- Record go/no‑go checkpoints as Jira milestones.
- Open bugs in Jira for production incidents and set severity.
- Reference Jira IDs in commit messages and PR descriptions (GitHub/Bitbucket).
- Log experiments to MLflow and tag runs with the Jira ticket and Git SHA.

Per §1.1, §1.2, §1.6, §4.1, §4.2 and §5.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What are the requirements for designing an A/B test?

inputState.inputText: What are the requirements for designing an A/B test?

message.text: What are the requirements for designing an A/B test?

response.value:
Direct answer: An A/B test must have a pre-registered hypothesis, a powered sample-size (≥80% power at α=0.05), randomized assignment, instrumented and validated data collection, a predefined analysis and stopping plan, MLflow tracking, peer review (senior review for high‑stakes), and monitoring plus a rollback/runbook.

What to do: Define hypothesis and primary KPI; Calculate sample size (power ≥80%, α=0.05); Pre-register hypothesis and analysis; Randomize and document stratification; Instrument and validate data collection; Log runs and Git SHA in MLflow; Peer-review design; Define stopping rules and rollback; Create monitoring dashboards.

Named tools: MLflow (Databricks), PowerBI/Grafana/Evidently AI.

Per §1.5 and §5.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What is pre-registration and when is it required?

inputState.inputText: What is pre-registration and when is it required?

message.text: What is pre-registration and when is it required?

response.value:
Direct answer: Pre-registration is formally recording your hypothesis and analysis plan before any data collection or peeking at outcomes; it is required before data collection for all experiments (including every A/B test) — no post‑hoc hypothesis formulation is allowed.  

What to do:
- Pre-register hypothesis in Jira or an experiment registry.
- Define primary/secondary outcomes, analysis plan, stopping rules, randomization, and data sources.
- Compute and record sample size & power analysis (≥80% power at α=0.05 for A/B tests).
- Log the experiment in MLflow (mlflow.start_run(); mlflow.log_param/metric/artifact) and tag with Git SHA and Jira link.
- Submit the pre‑registration for peer review (senior review if high‑stakes).

Per §1.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: When can we use causal language like 'X caused Y' in deli...

inputState.inputText: When can we use causal language like 'X caused Y' in deliverables?

message.text: When can we use causal language like 'X caused Y' in deliverables?

response.value:
Fetching the Brambles DS Standards guidance via the data-science-standards skill so the answer cites exact sections and gives actionable steps. Calling the standards skill and reporting intent in parallel.Direct answer: Use causal language only when a valid causal identification strategy is used (randomized experiment, diff‑in‑diff, regression discontinuity, or instrumental variables), the hypothesis was pre‑registered, the study is adequately powered, and the design and results have passed peer review; otherwise describe associations, not causation.

What to do:
1. Create a Jira story and pre‑register the hypothesis.
2. Design an identification strategy (randomized, diff‑in‑diff, RDD, or IV).
3. Run a power analysis (≥80% power, α=0.05).
4. Log runs to MLflow on Databricks and tag Git SHA.
5. Perform balance/parallel‑trends/instrument diagnostics and sensitivity checks.
6. Request peer review (senior review for high‑stakes).
7. Document methods, assumptions, limitations, and approved causal wording; produce a white paper if methodologically substantive.

Per §1.5 of the Brambles DS Standards (see also §§1.2, 5.1, 3.2).

data.customTitle: What is the standard project directory structure for a DS...

inputState.inputText: What is the standard project directory structure for a DS repository?

message.text: What is the standard project directory structure for a DS repository?

response.value:
Per §2.3 of the Brambles DS Standards: the canonical repo layout is:

project-name/
- README.md
- setup.py or pyproject.toml
- src/project_name/
  - data/
  - features/
  - models/
  - utils/
- tests/
  - unit/
  - integration/
- notebooks/        ← exploratory only, never production
- configs/
- docs/
- requirements.txt or environment.yml

Specific notes: put production code in src/, keep notebooks exploratory only, include tests (pytest) and type hints, and enforce formatting (Black, 88-char) and naming conventions (snake_case, CamelCase, ALL_CAPS) per §§2.1–2.4.

What to do:
- Create the directory tree
- Add README.md and setup.py or pyproject.toml
- Move production code into src/project_name/
- Create tests/unit and tests/integration and run pytest
- Add requirements.txt or environment.yml
- Configure Black (88-char) and a linter

data.customTitle: When should a white paper be written instead of a slide d...

inputState.inputText: When should a white paper be written instead of a slide deck?

message.text: When should a white paper be written instead of a slide deck?

response.value:
Direct answer: Write a white paper (not a slide deck) when the work contains significant mathematical or novel methodological content, must be reproducible/publishable, or supports high‑stakes decisions requiring full derivations and statistical analysis. Per §3.2.

What to do:
- Create a formal white paper (LaTeX, Markdown→PDF, or Word) including problem formulation, math/notation, methodology, derivations, experimental design, results, statistical analysis, limitations, and future work.
- Log experiments to MLflow on Databricks and tag runs with the Git SHA.
- Add a model card documenting data, features, hyperparameters, benchmarks, and fairness.
- Submit the paper for peer review (include senior review for high‑stakes work).
- Archive the paper in the repo and link it from the Jira project charter.

Per §3.2 (see also §5.1, §3.3, §1.5) of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What docstring standard should we use and where are docst...

inputState.inputText: What docstring standard should we use and where are docstrings required?

message.text: What docstring standard should we use and where are docstrings required?

response.value:
Direct answer: Use a single, consistent docstring style (NumPy or Google) — recommended: NumPy — and require docstrings on all public functions, classes, and modules. Complex algorithms also need inline WHY comments. Per §3.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

What to do:
1. Choose NumPy style and record it in README/CONTRIBUTING.
2. Add docstrings to every public function, class, and module using the chosen style.
3. Add inline comments for complex algorithm rationale.
4. Configure CI to check for docstring presence/style and block merges if missing.
5. Enforce docstrings for production code alongside type hints (see §2.1). Per §3.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What branching strategy should DS teams follow in Git?

inputState.inputText: What branching strategy should DS teams follow in Git?

message.text: What branching strategy should DS teams follow in Git?

response.value:
Querying the Brambles DS Standards skill to give a standards-backed, citable recommendation and actionable steps. Calling the standards skill now (and reporting intent).Use a formal branching strategy: adopt Git Flow or trunk‑based development — keep main/master always deployable; use develop as the integration branch if using Git Flow; name feature and hotfix branches like feature/JIRA-123-description and hotfix/JIRA-456-description.

What to do:
1. Decide on Git Flow or trunk‑based development and document it in the repo README.  
2. Create branch protection rules in GitHub or Bitbucket to protect main (and develop if used).  
3. Require pull requests to change main/develop and enforce ≥1 peer review.  
4. Enforce Conventional Commits and include Jira IDs in branch names and commit messages.  
5. Configure CI/CD (Bitbucket Pipelines or your CI) to run linters, pytest, and checks; require passing status checks before merge.  
6. Tag releases and define a rollback/hotfix process.

Per §4.1–§4.4 of the Brambles DS Standards.

3. Add Jira ID and testing notes to the PR description.
4. Only merge once CI and reviews are green.

Per §4.2, §4.3, §4.4 and §2.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

- Pin dependencies and enforce reproducible Conda/Docker environments.
- Prohibit processing company data outside AWS.

data.customTitle: What annotation and data management standards apply to co...

inputState.inputText: What annotation and data management standards apply to computer vision projects?

message.text: What annotation and data management standards apply to computer vision projects?

response.value:
Direct answer: Use a dedicated annotation platform, version every image/annotation together, maintain a versioned labeling guideline, and run automated + multi-pass annotation quality checks.  

What to do:
1. Configure an annotation tool (Label Studio, CVAT, Labelbox, or Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth) and set up user roles.  
2. Version datasets (raw images, augmented copies, annotation files) with DVC or enable S3 bucket versioning; push to remote.  
3. Create and commit a labeling_guidelines.md in the repo; include examples and edge cases.  
4. Implement QA: compute inter-annotator agreement (e.g., kappa/IoU), run consistency-validation scripts, and require multi-pass review.  
5. Record dataset provenance (manifests, checksums, dataset version) and reference it in experiment tracking.  
6. Detect/redact PII and perform a privacy-impact check for images of people.

Per §7.1 (Data & Annotation Management) of the Brambles DS Standards.

message.text: What ethical and privacy requirements apply to CV projects involving images of people?

response.value:
Per §7.5 of the Brambles DS Standards: CV projects with images of people must perform a privacy impact assessment, detect/redact PII, and document dataset biases; they must also meet model-card and experiment-tracking requirements (§3.3, §5.1).

What to do:

- Implement PII detection and redaction (face blurring, license‑plate obfuscation).

- Version datasets with DVC or S3 versioning.
- Log experiments and sample predictions to MLflow (tag runs with Git SHA).
- Document biases and limitations and create a model card.
- Configure drift/performance monitoring (Evidently AI, CloudWatch).
- Store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager / SSM / Bitbucket / Databricks Secrets.

Per §7.5, §7.1, §3.3, §5.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

Direct answer: A gated, auditable pipeline that registers an MLflow model, validates it (tests + data checks + peer review), promotes it to Staging for canary/shadow deployment, monitors drift/SLAs in production, and either promotes to Production or rolls back — with model card, SLA, and rollback plan attached.

What to do
- Log experiment to MLflow with params, metrics, artifacts, and Git SHA.
- Register model in MLflow Model Registry as a version.
- Create and attach a model card documenting data, features, hyperparams, limits.

- Request peer review and approval in PR/Jira.
- Promote to Staging via CI/CD and deploy (Databricks Model Serving or container on ECS/EKS/Kubeflow).
- Monitor performance and drift (Evidently, CloudWatch); capture predictions/outcomes.
- Promote to Production or execute rollback plan; archive old versions.

response.value:
Running a standards lookup and producing a concise, actionable answer per the Brambles DS Standards (to cite sections and provide step-by-step "What to do"). Calling intent and the DS-standards skill.Direct answer: Trigger retraining by a deployment-time defined cadence or automated drift alerts, and manage it with automated retraining pipelines plus human-in-the-loop approval and clear rollback/runbook procedures. Per §1.4 and §5.5.

What to do:
- Define retraining triggers: schedule cadence and drift thresholds; record in the model card.
- Set up monitoring: enable Evidently AI + CloudWatch/Databricks monitoring and log metrics to MLflow.
- Create retraining pipelines: implement Databricks Jobs (or Step Functions/Glue) parametrized with dataset versions and Git SHA.
- Add gates: require human approval in the MLflow/Databricks Model Registry before production promotion.
- Use canary/shadow deploys and automate rollback plans.
- Capture predictions/outcomes to Delta Lake/S3 and log MLflow runs.
- Add data validation (Great Expectations) and CI checks; track runbooks and incidents in Jira.

Per §1.4 and §5.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.
